title: Ultrasound-guided pectoral nerve block is an effective analgesic alternative to thoracic paravertebral block for breast cancer surgery: A randomised controlled trial.
abstract: BACKGROUND AND AIMS: The pectoral nerve (PECS) block is a viable alternative to the thoracic paravertebral block (TPVB) for providing analgesia in breast cancer surgery.
reasoning: Model1: Based on the title and abstract, the study directly compares ultrasound-guided pectoral nerve block with thoracic paravertebral block for analgesia in breast cancer surgery, which aligns with the PICO-TT criteria. The study is a randomized controlled trial, providing strong evidence for the comparison between the two techniques. | Model2: The study meets all the criteria of the PICO-TT framework. It includes patients undergoing breast cancer surgery, compares the effectiveness of ultrasound-guided pectoral nerve block to thoracic paravertebral block as an analgesic alternative, and assesses outcomes related to analgesia. The study type is a randomized controlled trial which is suitable for systematic review.

title: Effect of Vitamin D Supplementation on Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
abstract: CONTEXT: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with metabolic and endocrine disorders in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
reasoning: Model1: The study title and abstract clearly state that it is a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluating the effect of vitamin D supplementation on PCOS, which aligns with the research question of interest. The abstract also mentions the population (patients with PCOS), intervention (vitamin D supplementation), and outcomes (metabolic and endocrine disorders) are relevant to the PICO-TT criteria. The study types are specified as randomized controlled trials, which match the inclusion criteria. | Model2: The study title and abstract clearly state that the research is focused on the effect of Vitamin D supplementation on Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS), which aligns with the intervention and outcomes criteria of the PICO-TT. The study also involves randomized controlled trials, meeting the study types criteria. Therefore, this study should be included for further evaluation.
